retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025

The Latent Space newsletter surveys the agentic AI platform landscape as of mid-2025. The analysis identifies three dominant approaches: (1) orchestration frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph) focused on multi-agent coordination; (2) RAG-first platforms (LlamaIndex, Vertex AI Search) focused on grounding agents in enterprise data; and (3) evaluation-native platforms (Langfuse, Braintrust, PromptLayer) focused on reliability and measurement. The newsletter argues that evaluation will become the key differentiator as models commoditize.

retrieved_docs.title: Imbue Blog — a-healthy-ecosystem-for-ai-agents-introducing-the-afi
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Imbue Blog — a-healthy-ecosystem-for-ai-agents-introducing-the-afi

At Imbue, we believe that AI agents will radically reshape society in the coming decades. In this future, a healthy ecosystem of open agents and software will be necessary for individuals to have control over the technologies that shape our lives.
Building toward this future motivates our work. It’s why we released Sculptor, with the ambition for it to be the best way to build, modify, and run agents — when the barrier to creating and sharing open agents drops, everyone can benefit from them. It’s why we also work on the conditions that sustain open agents, including business models for open software and fundamental machine learning research to make neural networks understandable.
The future for AI agents isn’t guaranteed. The decisions that we make now, about the things we build (and not build) and the rules that govern them, will shape the world they enter and transform. We need good judgment, humility, and the ability to hold meaningful conversations between builders and policymakers to make these choices wisely.
Last week we joined Anthropic, Intuit, Edgerunner AI, Unstructured, Shift5, and Lilt AI to launch the Agentic Futures Initiative (AFI) to help shape clear, practical, a

retrieved_docs.title: TechCrunch: Agentic AI Funding Surge in Early 2026
retrieved_docs.snippet:
TechCrunch: Agentic AI Funding Surge in Early 2026

TechCrunch reports that agentic AI startups raised over $4B in Q1 2026, with the majority of deals concentrated in orchestration infrastructure, enterprise RAG platforms, and agent evaluation tooling. Notable rounds include CrewAI's $100M Series B, a $60M raise by Composio, and continued growth at Langfuse. Investors cite the shift from single-turn chatbots to persistent, multi-step agents as the key driver of demand.

retrieved_docs.title: LangChain Blog — Deep Agents
retrieved_docs.snippet:
LangChain Blog — Deep Agents

Using an LLM to call tools in a loop is the simplest form of an agent. This architecture, however, can yield agents that are “shallow” and fail to plan and act over longer, more complex tasks. Applications like “Deep Research”, “Manus”, and “Claude Code” have gotten around this limitation by implementing a combination of four things: a planning tool, sub agents, access to a file system, and a detailed prompt.
Acknowledgements: this exploration was primarily inspired by Claude Code and reports of people using it for more than just coding. What about Claude Code made it general purpose, and could we abstract out and generalize those characteristics?
Deep agents in the wild
The dominant agent architecture to emerge is also the simplest: running in a loop, calling tools.
Doing this naively, however, leads to agents that are a bit shallow. “Shallow” here refers to the agents inability to plan over longer time horizons and do more complex tasks.
Research and coding have emerged as two areas where agents have been created that buck this trend. All of the major model providers have an agent for Deep Research and for “async” coding tasks. Many startups and customers are creating versions of

retrieved_docs.title: Import AI — Import AI 447: The AGI economy; testing AIs with generated games; and agent ecologies
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Import AI — Import AI 447: The AGI economy; testing AIs with generated games; and agent ecologies

Import AI 447: The AGI economy; testing AIs with generated games; and agent ecologies
by Jack Clark
Welcome to Import AI, a newsletter about AI research. Import AI runs on arXiv and feedback from readers. If you’d like to support this, please subscribe.
The AGI economy – most labor goes to the machines, and humans shift to verification:
…What grappling with the singularity seriously looks like…
Researchers with MIT, WashU, and UCLA have written a fun paper called “Some Simple Economics of AGI” which wrestles with what happens when machines can do the vast majority of tasks in the economy. The conclusion is that our ability as humans to control and benefit from this vast machine-driven economy will rely on allocating our ability toward monitoring and verifying the actions of our myriad AI agents, and indulging in artisanal tasks where the value comes from the human-derived aspect more than any particular capability.
What is AGI in an economic sense? “We model the AGI transition as the collision of two racing cost curves: an exponentially decaying Cost to Automate and a biologically bottlenecked Cost to Verify,” the authors write. “In an economy where autonomous agents act with broad

retrieved_docs.title: Tavily — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Tavily — about

/about us
The agentic web is vast and unstructured. Tavily delivers high-quality, fresh, and structured web context, making it easy for agents to stay grounded as they scale from prototypes to production.
/our mission
Our mission is to help AI agents navigate the web quickly and efficiently by shaping the context they see. We prioritize relevance, freshness, and efficiency so agents reason accurately without wasting tokens.
/team
We’re a team of collaborators, builders, and deep thinkers, obsessed with making reliable web access possible for AI agents in the real world. We move quickly, support each other, and stay focused on shipping systems that deliver real impact in production.

retrieved_docs.title: Humanloop — humanloop.com
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Humanloop — humanloop.com

We are thrilled to announce that the Humanloop team is joining Anthropic!
From the start, our mission at Humanloop has been to enable the safe and rapid adoption of AI. Now, as the pace of AI progress accelerates, we think Anthropic is the ideal home to amplify our impact.
We've had the privilege of working with customers who continually push the boundaries of AI adoption. We owe a huge debt of gratitude for their trust and invaluable feedback along the way. As we sunset the Humanloop platform, we will continue to work closely with our customers to make their transition as smooth as possible.
We are grateful to our investors: Albion, Index, YC, Local Globe, UCLTF and many fantastic angels. Building this company has been an incredible journey and we were fortunate to have investors who trusted and supported us at every step.
Reflecting on our journey, we're immensely proud of what we built. We were the first development platform for LLM applications and we shaped industry standards for how to manage and evaluate AI. We're excited about this next chapter, joining Anthropic to help build an AI future that benefits everyone.
Raza, Jordan, Peter
and the whole Humanloop team

retrieved_docs.title: Weaviate Blog — weaviate-agent-skills
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Weaviate Blog — weaviate-agent-skills

It has never been easier to build software. With tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot, we’ve moved into a high-velocity workflow often called ‘vibe coding.’ You describe a feature, an agent blueprints the logic, and the app comes to life.
But this speed usually hits a wall when it meets specialized infrastructure. While agents are great at general boilerplate, they struggle with the nuances of working with specialized tools. They often hallucinate legacy v3 Weaviate syntax, guess at hybrid search alpha parameters, or fail to implement efficient multivector embedding strategies. When you’re trying to manage three parallel agentic workflows, you don't have time to be a full-time debugger for an agent’s hallucinations. You need the implementation to be right on the first try.
To solve this, we are introducing Weaviate Agent Skills. This repository acts as a bridge between the most popular coding agents and Weaviate’s infrastructure.
Agent Skills has become one of the most popular tools to help coding agents write better code. The Agent Skills format, developed by Anthropic, works with Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, Gemini CLI, and many other tools. Our new

retrieved_docs.title: MultiOn Blog — agi-inc-visa
retrieved_docs.snippet:
MultiOn Blog — agi-inc-visa

We believe the way people shop and transact online is about to change. In the near future most purchases won’t start with a search box or an app. They’ll start with an agent acting on your behalf. From booking flights and hotels to ordering groceries or buying a gift, your agent will handle the details so you don’t have to.
For this shift to work, every action needs to be reliable. A missed reservation or failed payment isn’t just an inconvenience, it erodes trust. That’s why we’re partnering with Visa to make agent-led transactions seamless, secure with close to 100% reliability.
AI agents today can answer questions and draft emails, but when it comes to commerce the stakes are higher. Money moves. Commitments are made. A single error can break the experience.
Reliability is what unlocks confidence. Without it, agentic commerce won’t scale:
Our REAL Eval high-fidelity environments test agents against real-world conditions. This is how we ensure agents perform with precision and reliability where it matters most.
With Visa, we’re embedding trust directly into these agent flows. Visa’s expertise in secure payments paired with our agent infrastructure means every booking, purchase an
